# UC-14.5.18 · HiveMQ Retained Message Store Growth

## Description

Retained messages accumulate with misconfigured publishers; growth risks disk exhaustion and slower broker startup, especially at OT edge with constrained storage.

## Value

Retained messages accumulate with misconfigured publishers; growth risks disk exhaustion and slower broker startup, especially at OT edge with constrained storage.

## Implementation

Map the exact HiveMQ metric name from your Prometheus/SVA mapping. Alert on week-over-week growth or crossing a capacity threshold. Correlate spikes with new devices publishing retained messages on unique topics.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: HiveMQ Splunk Extension (metrics to Splunk).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ot` `sourcetype="hivemq:metrics"` retained message counters.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Map the exact HiveMQ metric name from your Prometheus/SVA mapping. Alert on week-over-week growth or crossing a capacity threshold. Correlate spikes with new devices publishing retained messages on unique topics.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot sourcetype="hivemq:metrics"
| eval m=lower(coalesce(metric_name, metric, name))
| where match(m, "retain")
| eval v=coalesce(value, metric_value, _value)
| bin _time span=1h
| stats max(v) as retained_max by host, _time
| timechart span=1h max(retained_max) by host
```

Understanding this SPL

**HiveMQ Retained Message Store Growth** — Retained messages accumulate with misconfigured publishers; growth risks disk exhaustion and slower broker startup, especially at OT edge with constrained storage.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ot` `sourcetype="hivemq:metrics"` retained message counters. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): HiveMQ Splunk Extension (metrics to Splunk). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot; **sourcetype**: hivemq:metrics. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ot, sourcetype="hivemq:metrics". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **m** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where match(m, "retain")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **v** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1h** buckets with a separate series **by host** — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.

Step 3 — Validate
Run the search for a known busy window, then compare counts and clients with the HiveMQ control center or broker metrics. Reconcile top clients and topics with what the broker shows.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (retained count over time), Area chart (growth rate), Single value (current max), Table (brokers over threshold).

## SPL

```spl
index=ot sourcetype="hivemq:metrics"
| eval m=lower(coalesce(metric_name, metric, name))
| where match(m, "retain")
| eval v=coalesce(value, metric_value, _value)
| bin _time span=1h
| stats max(v) as retained_max by host, _time
| timechart span=1h max(retained_max) by host
```

## Visualization

Line chart (retained count over time), Area chart (growth rate), Single value (current max), Table (brokers over threshold).
